core_intuitions: Attention is O(N^2) because it wires every token to every earlier token; the alternatives replace all-pairs wiring with a single fixed-size rolling state updated left-to-right, making cost O(N) and per-step inference O(1).

Linear attention's kernel trick is pure reassociation: computing phi(K)^T V FIRST collapses the giant N x N score matrix into a tiny r x d_v state you compute once and reuse, turning O(N^2) into O(N).

The train-parallel / infer-recurrent duality: the SAME computation has a parallel matmul form (GPU-fast during training) and an O(1)-per-step recurrent form (constant memory at inference), stitched together by the chunkwise scan (dense matmul inside a chunk, carry a low-rank state between chunks).

Sparse / sliding-window attention keeps the softmax but restricts WHICH keys each query sees (banded, block-sparse, global+local), cutting O(N^2) to O(N*W); stacking L such layers compounds the effective receptive field to ~L*W.
Mamba's selectivity makes B, C and Delta input-dependent, letting the model choose what to write into and read from the fixed state (Delta ~ 0 to skip a token, Delta large to write it strongly).
Convergence: SSMs, linear attention, RWKV and RetNet are all one gated linear recurrence S_t = G_t (elementwise) S_{t-1} + k_t v_t^T, differing only in how the gate/decay G_t is parameterized.

existing.name: ssm-scan
existing.improvement: Renders correctly and legibly in both light and dark themes. It nails core intuition #1 (a single rolling fixed-size state swept left-to-right in O(N) steps vs attention's O(N^2) all-pairs wiring), with clean color separation of inputs / state / outputs and honest illustrative counts (4 steps vs 10 pairs). No clipping, no clutter, strong metaphor. Leave as-is.

new_figures.name: linear-attention-reassociation
new_figures.concept: The kernel trick as reassociation: (phi(Q) phi(K)^T) V  vs  phi(Q) (phi(K)^T V) -- the second order never builds the N x N matrix.
new_figures.rationale: This is THE mechanism of the entire linear-attention / SSM / RWKV / RetNet family and currently has no figure. The reader must SEE that moving the parentheses replaces a huge N x N score matrix with a small r x d_v state computed once. Without this picture, 'linear attention is O(N)' stays a claim; with it, it becomes obvious. Foundational for every downstream section (chunked form, Mamba-2 SSD, GLA).
new_figures.placement: Section 'Linear Attention: The Kernel Trick', right after 'The Reformulation' subsection (after the Linear-Attention equation and the sentence 'compute phi(K)^T V first', around the paragraph ending 'Total is O(N) rather than O(N^2).')
new_figures.brief: A side-by-side of two computation orders that produce the SAME output N x d_v, contrasting the object built in the middle. Use small illustrative shapes: N=6 tokens, feature dim r=3, value dim d_v=4. Draw each matrix as a small grid of cells (rows x cols) with a shape label underneath. TOP PATH, header 'Naive order: (phi(Q) phi(K)^T) V' in a caution/warn color: phi(Q) [6x3] times phi(K)^T [3x6] with a big arrow into a prominently large square grid labeled 'scores  N x N' (6x6) -- draw this square noticeably bigger and fill it in the warn color to read as 'the expensive object'; annotate it 'materialized: O(N^2) memory & FLOPs'. Then that 6x6 times V [6x4] -> output [6x4]. BOTTOM PATH, header 'Kernel order: phi(Q) (phi(K)^T V)' in a good/accent color: first bracket phi(K)^T [3x6] times V [6x4] with an arrow into a SMALL box labeled 'state  r x d_v' (3x4), drawn deliberately tiny and in the good color, annotated 'computed once, reused for every query'; then phi(Q) [6x3] times that small [3x4] -> output [6x4]. Put a centered brace/equals between the two paths reading 'same result, associativity moves the parentheses'. Cost tags: top 'O(N^2 d)', bottom 'O(N r d)'. The single most important visual signal is size + color: the N x N square is big and warn-colored (avoid it), the r x d_v state is tiny and good-colored (the whole point). All colors via theme vars (var(--accent), var(--good), var(--warn), var(--muted), var(--ink-soft)); everything inside the viewBox with clear left-to-right reading order and no overlapping grids.

new_figures.name: sparse-attention-mask-family
new_figures.concept: Four attention masks over the same 7-token causal sequence: full, sliding-window, block-sparse, global+local -- what each query is allowed to see and the resulting per-query cost.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter conveys this only as a monospace ASCII table. A clean grid infographic makes the central sparse-attention intuition click instantly -- 'every query still runs a softmax, we just prune which keys it sees' -- and visually grounds the cost table (keys-per-query -> O(N*W) etc.). It also sets up the receptive-field-grows-with-depth and global-layer arguments that recur in the hybrids section. High intuition-per-pixel; replaces functional-but-plain ASCII.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Sparse Attention Patterns', replacing / directly beside the ASCII code block (the 'Legend: X = attention computed ...' block) and above the 'Keys per query / Cost per layer' table.
new_figures.brief: Four small square heatmap grids in a row, each 7x7 with query index on the vertical axis (q0..q6 top-to-bottom) and key index on the horizontal (k0..k6). A filled cell = attention computed (accent color), an empty/muted cell = masked/skipped. All four are causal (nothing above the diagonal). PANEL 1 'Full causal': entire lower triangle filled; caption 'keys/query ~ N, cost O(N^2 d)'. PANEL 2 'Sliding window (W=3)': only a band of width 3 on and below the diagonal filled (positions t-2..t); caption 'keys/query = W, cost O(N W d)'. PANEL 3 'Block-sparse': same lower triangle but grouped into 2x2 or larger tile blocks where whole blocks are either fully filled or skipped (show a couple of skipped off-diagonal blocks as empty) to make the 'we keep computed tiles dense for tensor cores' point; caption 'block granularity keeps tiles dense'. PANEL 4 'Global + local': column k0 fully filled for every query (the global token, mark its header 'G'), PLUS a width-2 local band near the diagonal; the middle is empty; caption 'keys/query = g + W'. Below the row, a slim annotation strip: an icon of stacked layers with the note 'stack L sliding-window layers -> effective receptive field ~ L*W (info hops one window per layer)'. Keep cell size generous enough that 7x7 grids are legible; label the diagonal subtly. Theme-safe: filled cells var(--accent) (or a low-opacity accent fill), masked cells var(--v-grid)/muted, the global column can use var(--accent2) to distinguish it, text var(--ink-soft)/var(--muted). No hardcoded hex primaries; everything inside the viewBox with equal spacing between the four panels.

new_figures.name: gated-linear-recurrence-convergence
new_figures.concept: One recurrence, four architectures: S_t = G_t (elementwise) S_{t-1} + k_t v_t^T fans out to Linear Attention, RetNet, RWKV and Mamba by different choices of the gate G_t.
new_figures.rationale: The 'everything is a gated linear recurrence' unification is the chapter's intellectual payoff -- it retroactively ties linear attention, RetNet, RWKV, and Mamba/SSD into a single family. A fan diagram makes the convergence graspable at a glance and turns four separately-introduced sections into one idea, which is exactly the kind of structural intuition a static infographic conveys better than prose.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Gated Linear Attention and the Convergence', right after the GLA recurrence equations (S_t = G_t (elementwise) S_{t-1} + k_t^T v_t ; o_t = q_t S_t) and the line 'Different choices of G_t recover different architectures'.
new_figures.brief: A centered 'source' node fanning out to four leaves. TOP center: a rounded box (accent border) holding the master recurrence, two lines: 'S_t = G_t (odot) S_{t-1} + k_t v_t^T' and 'o_t = q_t S_t', titled 'Gated linear recurrence' with subtitle 'one update, one readout -- the gate G_t is the only thing that changes'. From this box, four connector lines fan downward to four leaf cards arranged left-to-right, each leaf a small pill showing the gate choice on top and the architecture name (bold) below: LEAF 1 gate 'G_t = I  (no decay)' -> 'Linear Attention'; LEAF 2 gate 'G_t = diag(gamma), constant' -> 'RetNet'; LEAF 3 gate 'G_t = diag(exp(-exp(w))), input scalar per channel' -> 'RWKV-style'; LEAF 4 gate 'G_t = diag(exp(Delta_t A)), input-dependent' -> 'Mamba / selective SSM'. Order the leaves along an implied axis from 'no gating' (left) to 'fully input-dependent gating' (right); add a faint horizontal arrow beneath labeled 'gate expressiveness / cost ->' to convey that reading direction. Keep each gate expression on a single line, small mono font; architecture names in the sans bold. Use var(--accent) for the source box and connectors, var(--ink-soft) for gate text, a subtle var(--muted) axis arrow. Ensure the four leaves are evenly spaced and none of their gate labels overflow their pill or collide -- allow two text lines per pill if needed. Fully inside the viewBox, symmetric fan.

new_figures.name: kv-cache-vs-state-decode-memory
new_figures.concept: Decode-time memory divergence: a transformer's KV cache grows one block per generated token (O(N d)) while an SSM/RWKV keeps a single fixed-size state (O(d N_s)) no matter how long the sequence gets.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's headline practical payoff -- 'the SSM inference advantage is most pronounced past 32K tokens', a worked example contrasting a towering KV cache against a constant state. The ssm-scan figure shows compute wiring, not memory-over-decode-steps; this fills the distinct and arguably most decision-relevant intuition (why anyone deploys these). Motion is genuinely useful: the KV stack visibly growing token-by-token against an unchanging state box is the whole point, so a long-animation earns its keep while staying legible as a static mid-decode frame.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The FLOP / Memory Trade-off Table', beside or after the '!!! example "Worked example: inference memory comparison at 32K context"' admonition.
new_figures.brief: A decode timeline shared by two panels, with a small 'decode step t' counter / progress ticker along the top that advances as tokens are generated. LEFT panel 'Transformer (attention)': a vertical KV cache drawn as a stack of small identical blocks that GROWS by one block every decode step -- start with a few blocks and animate it rising into a tall tower as t increases; label the tower 'KV cache: 2 * N * d' with a running note 'grows every step -> O(N d), unbounded'. Use the warn color for the tower. RIGHT panel 'SSM / Mamba (and RWKV)': a SINGLE fixed-size box labeled 'state  d x N_s' that stays exactly the same size as identical input tokens stream past it (show tokens entering and being absorbed, box unchanged); label 'O(d N_s), constant -- independent of N', in the good color. Between them a divider and a headline 'same tokens generated, very different memory'. The static frame (what a non-animating viewer sees) must be a MID-to-LATE decode moment: the KV tower already tall (with a faint ghost outline of its earlier shorter self and an up-arrow to imply it kept growing) next to the unchanged small state box -- so the grows-vs-constant contrast reads instantly without motion. Keep all magnitudes SCHEMATIC and qualitative: label the contrast as 'tens of GB vs a few MB at long context' as descriptive words only, do NOT bake in specific benchmark numbers or bar heights that imply measured values; small illustrative block counts are fine. Theme-safe colors via vars (var(--warn) for the growing cache, var(--good) for the constant state, var(--accent2) for streaming tokens, var(--muted) for labels and the ghost outline). Everything inside the viewBox; the tower must not overflow the top edge at its tallest animated height -- cap its height and let the 'up-arrow / unbounded' annotation carry the 'keeps going' meaning.

summary: The chapter is concept-dense (~1100 lines spanning quadratic attention, sparse/sliding-window, linear attention, S4/Mamba, RWKV, RetNet, Mamba-2 SSD, GLA and hybrids) but served by a single figure (ssm-scan), which is excellent and kept as-is. I propose 4 new figures targeting the foundational mechanisms that currently lack visuals: (1) the linear-attention kernel trick as reassociation -- the N x N score matrix collapsing into a tiny reusable r x d_v state (P1, the core of the whole family); (2) a clean sparse-attention mask-family grid to replace the ASCII (P1); (3) a fan diagram of the gated-linear-recurrence convergence tying linear-attention/RetNet/RWKV/Mamba into one framework (P2, the chapter's unifying payoff); and (4) a decode-time memory divergence animation showing a growing KV cache vs a constant SSM state (P2, the headline deployment intuition). All briefs are theme-safe, use small illustrative integers only, and are legible as static frames.
